title: Selected-language readiness

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Selected-language readiness; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: DSA implementation begins only after the learner can express functions, collections, recursion, tests, and complexity in one chosen language.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A learner traces an array algorithm correctly but first demonstrates safe indexing and functions in C++ before coding it. Your goal is to pass an implementation diagnostic or receive a targeted prerequisite remediation map. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: pf problem pseudocode; pf tools debug test; pf abstraction recursion. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: DSA implementation begins only after the learner can express functions, collections, recursion, tests, and complexity in one chosen language. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: Express the specification, invariant, proof, and asymptotic bound independently first; then implement in C with explicit bounds/ownership, C++ with value and library guarantees, Java with typed collections/equality, or Python with dynamic objects and library semantics.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Concept knowledge can transfer, but syntax, libraries, ownership, equality, and error behavior must be retested after a language switch. The draft therefore rejects: Knowing DSA concepts removes the need for implementation-language prerequisites.

includes: Demonstrate functions, sequences, recursion, testing and debugging in the selected language before DSA implementation.
Pass an implementation diagnostic or receive a targeted prerequisite remediation map.

Express the specification, invariant, proof, and asymptotic bound independently first; then implement in C with explicit bounds/ownership, C++ with value and library guarantees, Java with typed collections/equality, or Python with dynamic objects and library semantics.
excludes: Concept knowledge can transfer, but syntax, libraries, ownership, equality, and error behavior must be retested after a language switch.

outcomes: Pass an implementation diagnostic or receive a targeted prerequisite remediation map.

examples.title: Selected-language readiness: language-neutral trace
examples.situation: A learner traces an array algorithm correctly but first demonstrates safe indexing and functions in C++ before coding it.
walkthrough: Step 1 — State the abstract input and invariant: A learner traces an array algorithm correctly but first demonstrates safe indexing and functions in C++ before coding it.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Keep conceptual mastery, but verify language-specific implementation readiness before coding assessed DSA solutions.
examples.result: The conceptual result follows because Keep conceptual mastery, but verify language-specific implementation readiness before coding assessed DSA solutions.

examples.title: Selected-language readiness: implementation transfer
examples.situation: Express the specification, invariant, proof, and asymptotic bound independently first; then implement in C with explicit bounds/ownership, C++ with value and library guarantees, Java with typed collections/equality, or Python with dynamic objects and library semantics.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Keep the same specification and invariant across all four languages.

Step 2 — Map storage, library, equality, bounds, ownership, and error rules using: Express the specification, invariant, proof, and asymptotic bound independently first; then implement in C with explicit bounds/ownership, C++ with value and library guarantees, Java with typed collections/equality, or Python with dynamic objects and library semantics.
Step 3 — Retest this boundary in the selected implementation: Concept knowledge can transfer, but syntax, libraries, ownership, equality, and error behavior must be retested after a language switch.
examples.result: Concept mastery transfers, while implementation evidence remains language-specific.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Knowing DSA concepts removes the need for implementation-language prerequisites.
misconceptions.correction: Keep conceptual mastery, but verify language-specific implementation readiness before coding assessed DSA solutions.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use an invariant or counterexample to explain why this DSA belief fails: Knowing DSA concepts removes the need for implementation-language prerequisites.

analogy.example: A carefully labeled physical arrangement can introduce Selected-language readiness, while the canonical invariant and operations remain the actual algorithmic model.
usefulFor: Introducing structure, order, connectivity, or state transition before formal notation
limitations: A physical arrangement does not reproduce asymptotic cost, aliasing, numeric limits, recursion depth, or the four languages' implementation rules.
Concept knowledge can transfer, but syntax, libraries, ownership, equality, and error behavior must be retested after a language switch.

recap.summary: You should now be able to pass an implementation diagnostic or receive a targeted prerequisite remediation map. Keep this boundary in view: Concept knowledge can transfer, but syntax, libraries, ownership, equality, and error behavior must be retested after a language switch.
retrievalPrompts: State the Selected-language readiness invariant and complexity without code.
Why is this claim false: Knowing DSA concepts removes the need for implementation-language prerequisites.
Map checkpoint 'implementation readiness' to C, C++, Java, and Python.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Algorithm specification and invariants”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
